facts.text: Augustine was Bishop of Hippo.
Augustine wrote the Confessions.
Augustine believes God is great and infinitely wise.
Augustine believes human hearts are restless until they rest in God.
Augustine believes God made humans for himself.
Augustine had faith given to him by God through the Incarnation of God's Son.
Augustine was preached to.
Augustine believes he exists in God.
Augustine believes God fills heaven and earth.
Augustine believes God is unchangeable.
Augustine believes God is merciful and just.
Augustine was born of a mother and father.
Augustine was nursed by women who gave him milk.
Augustine learned to speak as a boy.
Augustine did not remember his infancy.
Augustine observed other infants to understand his own infancy.
Augustine as an infant cried when displeased.
Augustine as an infant smiled in sleep and while waking.
Augustine as an infant was indignant when not obeyed.
Augustine as an infant expressed himself through cries and gestures.
Augustine learned language by hearing words and observing what they referred to.
Augustine learned to speak by practicing sounds in his memory.
Augustine was subject to parental authority as a child.
Augustine was subject to the authority of his elders as a child.
Augustine believes infants are capable of envy.
Augustine believes infants have sinful inclinations.
Augustine believes he was shaped in iniquity.
Augustine believes his mother conceived him in sin.
Augustine transitioned from infancy to boyhood.
Augustine became a speaking boy after being a speechless infant.
Augustine learned words and eloquence from theatrical texts like those of Terence.
Augustine was taught that eloquence was necessary to gain one's ends or maintain opinions.
Augustine learned about mythological tales such as Jupiter's seduction of Danae from theatrical performances.
Augustine was beaten if he did not learn the assigned material.
Augustine was assigned to recite the words of Juno from a poetic work.
Augustine's declamation was applauded above that of many of his own age and class.
Augustine wasted his wit and tongue on empty trifles rather than on praising God.
Augustine was carried away to vanities and went out from God's presence.
Augustine observed men who were praised for relating their disordered lives in rich and adorned discourse.
Augustine's affections were darkened and he was removed from God.
Augustine feared committing a barbarism in speech more than committing moral wrongs.
Augustine deceived his tutor, masters, and parents with innumerable lies.
Augustine stole from his parents' cellar and table.
Augustine gave stolen goods to boys in exchange for play.
Augustine sought unfair conquests in play and desired preeminence.
Augustine was enslaved by greediness in his childhood.
Augustine had an implanted providence over his well-being as a child.
Augustine had a vigorous memory as a child.
Augustine was gifted with speech as a child.
Augustine was soothed by friendship as a child.
Augustine avoided pain, baseness, and ignorance as a child.
Augustine sought pleasures, sublimities, and truths in creatures rather than in God.
Augustine burned in his youth to be satiated in things below.
Augustine grew wild with various and shadowy loves in his youth.
Augustine's beauty consumed away and he stank in God's eyes.
Augustine pleased himself and desired to please in the eyes of men.
Augustine did not keep the measure of love and confused love with lustfulness.
Augustine was deaf by the clanking of the chain of his mortality.
Augustine strayed further from God and was tossed about, wasted, and dissipated.
Augustine boiled over in his fornications.
Augustine wandered into fruitless seed-plots of sorrows with proud dejectedness and restless weariness.
Augustine was sixteen years old when the madness of lust took rule over him.
Augustine resigned himself wholly to lust at age sixteen.
Augustine's friends took no care by marriage to save his fall.
Augustine's friends cared only that he should learn to speak excellently and be a persuasive orator.
Augustine's studies were intermitted during his sixteenth year.
Augustine had journeyed to Madaura to learn grammar and rhetoric.
Augustine's father was a poor freeman of Thagaste.
Augustine's father provided for his journey to Carthage for further studies.
Augustine's father furnished his son with necessaries for a far journey for studies' sake.
Augustine's father had no concern how Augustine grew towards God or how chaste he was.
Augustine's father had no concern whether Augustine was barren to God's culture.
Augustine lived with his parents during his sixteenth year.
Augustine left school for a while due to a season of idleness caused by his parents' narrow fortunes.
Unclean desires grew rank over Augustine's head during his sixteenth year.
Augustine's father saw him at the baths growing towards manhood with restless youthfulness.
Augustine's father anticipated his descendants and rejoiced in the tumult of Augustine's senses.
Augustine's mother had begun to be God's temple in her breast.
Augustine's father was a Catechumen and recently so.
Augustine's mother was startled with holy fear and trembling about Augustine's future.
Augustine was not yet baptised.
Augustine's mother warned him privately with great anxiety not to commit fornication.
Augustine's mother warned him especially never to defile another man's wife.
Augustine thought his mother's advices were womanish.
Augustine was ashamed to obey his mother's advices.
Augustine ran headlong with blindness regarding chastity.
Augustine was ashamed of a less shamelessness among his equals.
Augustine took pleasure in the praise of his flagitiousness.
Augustine made himself worse than he was to avoid being dispraised.
Augustine would claim to have sinned when he had not in order to seem less contemptible.
Augustine walked the streets of Babylon and wallowed in the mire thereof.
Augustine was easy to be seduced.
Augustine's mother fled out of the centre of Babylon.
Augustine's mother went slowly in the skirts of Babylon.
Augustine's mother advised him to chastity.
Augustine would claim to have committed sins he had not committed to avoid seeming contemptible.
Augustine walked the streets of Babylon and wallowed in vice.
Augustine's mother fled from the center of Babylon but remained in its outskirts.
Augustine's mother feared a wife would hinder his hopes of learning.
Augustine's parents desired that he attain learning.
Augustine's father had little thought of God and held vain conceits about Augustine.
Augustine's mother believed learning would help him attain God.
Augustine was given excessive freedom and lacked proper discipline.
Augustine stole pears from a tree near his family's vineyard.
Augustine stole the pears not out of hunger or poverty but from excess and wickedness.
Augustine did not care to eat the stolen pears but threw them to hogs.
Augustine stole the pears primarily because the act was forbidden.
Augustine loved the theft itself rather than what was stolen.
Augustine committed the theft with a group of young companions.
Augustine would not have stolen the pears alone.
Augustine's pleasure in the theft came from the company of fellow-sinners.
Augustine loved the sport of deceiving those who disapproved.
Augustine came to Carthage as a young man.
Augustine loved to love before he actually loved anyone.
Augustine hated safety and sought a way with snares.
Augustine experienced a famine of inward spiritual food.
Augustine defiled friendship with lust and concupiscence.
Augustine fell into romantic love and desired to be ensnared by it.
Augustine's romantic love brought him jealousy, suspicions, fears, angers, and quarrels.
Augustine was carried away by stage-plays and theatrical performances.
Augustine watched tragic plays that depicted miseries similar to his own.
Augustine later regretted and confessed his youthful sins.
Augustine attributed his avoidance of greater evils to God's grace and guidance.
Augustine believed God's mercy forgave his sins.
Augustine was affected by theatrical passions and feigned emotions.
Augustine attended theatrical performances and rejoiced with lovers in their wickedness.
Augustine loved to grieve and sought out things to grieve about.
Augustine was straying from God's flock and became infected with spiritual disease.
Augustine pursued sacrilegious curiosity that led him away from God.
Augustine sacrificed his evil actions to devils.
Augustine was chief in the rhetoric school.
Augustine took pride and swelled with arrogancy in his position.
Augustine lived among the 'Subverters' who persecuted strangers with jeering.
Augustine abhorred the doings of the Subverters while living among them.
Augustine desired to be eminent in eloquence out of vainglory.
Augustine's father was dead two years before his nineteenth year.
Augustine received allowances from his mother during his nineteenth year.
Augustine read Cicero's 'Hortensius' which altered his affections.
Reading 'Hortensius' turned Augustine's prayers toward God.
Augustine longed with burning desire for immortality of wisdom after reading 'Hortensius'.
Augustine's tender heart had devoutly drunk in the name of Christ with his mother's milk.
Augustine resolved to bend his mind to the holy Scriptures.
Augustine found the Scriptures unworthy compared to Cicero's eloquence.
Augustine's swelling pride shrunk from the lowliness of Scripture.
Augustine disdained to be a little one and took himself to be great.
Augustine fell among men who were proudly doting and exceeding carnal.
Augustine sought Truth but was served false philosophies instead.
Augustine hungered and thirsted after Truth itself, not merely God's works.
Augustine was fed on empty husks and was not nourished.
Augustine believed in false elements and five dens of darkness.
Augustine sought God according to the sense of flesh rather than understanding of mind.
Augustine's soul dwelt abroad in the eye of his flesh.
Augustine was troubled by questions about the origin of evil.
Augustine did not know that evil was a privation of good.
Augustine's sight reached only to bodies and his mind only to phantasms.
Augustine did not know God to be a Spirit without bodily shape.
Augustine was ignorant of true inward righteousness.
Augustine did not understand that righteousness was the same always and everywhere.
Augustine was ignorant of the fitting application of divine law to different times and places.
Augustine did not understand how divine justice could prescribe different things at different times.
Augustine wrote verses.
Augustine censured the holy Fathers in his blindness.
Augustine believed that a fig-tree wept when it was plucked.
Augustine believed Manichaean saints could liberate particles of divinity through eating fruit.
Augustine showed more mercy to fruits of the earth than to hungry men.
Augustine's mother was a faithful believer who wept and prayed for him.
Augustine's mother had a vision of a shining youth telling her Augustine would be where she was.
Augustine wallowed in the mire of Manichaeism for almost nine years.
Augustine's mother was a chaste, godly, and sober widow.
A Bishop told Augustine's mother that Augustine was unteachable due to being puffed up with heresy.
The Bishop told Augustine's mother that Augustine would find the error of Manichaeism through reading.
The Bishop had himself been consigned to the Manichees as a child by his seduced mother.
The Bishop told Augustine's mother, 'the son of these tears should not perish.'
Augustine lived seduced and seducing for nine years from his nineteenth to his twenty-eighth year.
Augustine hunted after popular praise, theatrical applauses, and poetic prizes.
Augustine deceived his friends and was deceived by them.
Augustine taught rhetoric.
Augustine was overcome by cupidity in teaching rhetoric.
Augustine preferred honest scholars and taught them without artifice.
Augustine had one long-term partner in a non-lawful marriage.
Augustine remained faithful to his partner.
Augustine experienced the difference between marriage for procreation and lustful love through his relationship.
Augustine had a child with his partner against his will.
Augustine was asked by a wizard what he would give to win a theatrical prize.
Augustine refused to allow a fly to be killed for an imperishable gold garland.
Augustine rejected sacrifices to devils, though not from pure love of God.
Augustine did not know how to conceive of God beyond material brightness.
Augustine consulted astrologers without scruple.
Augustine believed astrologers seemed to use no sacrifice or prayer to spirits for divinations.
Augustine refused to allow a fly to be killed for him to win a garland.
Augustine rejected sacrifices offered to devils on his behalf.
Augustine did not initially know how to love God.
Augustine consulted mathematicians without scruple.
Augustine believed Christian piety rejects divination.
Augustine was advised by a wise physician to cast away books of nativity-casters.
The physician had studied astrology in his youth but abandoned it for medicine.
The physician found astrology utterly false.
Augustine had rhetoric as a profession to maintain himself by.
Nebridius was a youth singularly good and of holy fear.
Nebridius derided the whole body of divination.
Augustine began to teach rhetoric in his native town.
Augustine made a friend of his own age from a community of pursuits.
Augustine's friend had grown up with him as a school-fellow and play-fellow.
Augustine warped his friend from true faith to superstitious and pernicious fables.
Augustine's mother bewailed him for his superstitions.
Augustine's friend became very ill with a fever.
Augustine's friend was baptized while senseless in a death-sweat.
Augustine's friend recovered from the fever.
Augustine's friend rejected Augustine's jesting about his baptism.
Augustine's friend died a few days later from the fever.
Augustine's native country became a torment to him after his friend's death.
Augustine's father's house became a strange unhappiness to him.
Augustine hated all places because they did not contain his dead friend.
Augustine became a great riddle to himself.
Augustine found tears sweet because they succeeded his friend in his affections.
Augustine wept most bitterly after his friend's death.
Augustine found repose in bitterness.
Augustine held his wretched life dearer than his friend.
Augustine loathed exceedingly to live and feared to die.
Augustine hated death as a cruel enemy for bereaving him of his friend.
Augustine imagined death would speedily make an end of all men.
Augustine wondered that others subject to death lived when his loved friend was dead.
Augustine felt that his soul and his friend's soul were one soul in two bodies.
Augustine's life was a horror to him because he would not live halved.
Augustine fretted, sighed, wept, and was distracted after his friend's death.
Augustine bore about a shattered and bleeding soul.
Augustine found no repose in calm groves, games, music, fragrant spots, banquetings, bed, couch, books, or poetry.
Augustine found a little refreshment only in groaning and tears.
Augustine knew his load ought to have been raised to God but could not or would not do so.
Augustine's conception of God was not solid or substantial but a mere phantom.
Augustine fled from his native country Thagaste to Carthage.
Augustine was gradually restored by time and the solaces of other friends.
Augustine loved other friends instead of God.
Augustine loved talking and jesting together with friends.
Augustine loved doing kind offices by turns with friends.
Augustine loved reading together with friends.
Augustine loved playing the fool or being earnest together with friends.
Augustine loved dissenting without discontent with friends.
Augustine loved teaching and learning with friends.
Augustine loved longing for absent friends with impatience.
Augustine loved welcoming coming friends with joy.
Augustine believed that one's conscience condemns itself if one does not love him that loves again.
Augustine believed blessed is he who loves God and his friend in God and his enemy for God.
Augustine was approximately six or seven and twenty years old when he wrote volumes on the fair and fit.
Augustine wrote two or three books on the fair and fit.
Augustine dedicated his books to Hierius, an orator of Rome whom he had never met in person.
Augustine loved Hierius for his fame and learning without having seen him.
Augustine had heard some of Hierius's words that pleased him.
Augustine loved men based on the judgment of other men rather than on God's judgment.
Augustine would not have wanted to be commended or loved like actors are commended and loved.
Augustine preferred to be unknown rather than known in the way actors are known.
Augustine preferred to be hated rather than loved in the way actors are loved.
Augustine's love for Hierius was based more on the love of his commenders than on Hierius's actual qualities.
Augustine wrote about beauty and defined what is fair in itself and what is fit in correspondence to other things.
Augustine supported his definitions of beauty with corporeal examples.
Augustine turned his attention to the nature of the mind but was hindered by false notions of spiritual things.
Augustine could not see incorporeal substance and therefore thought he could not see his mind.
Augustine loved peace in virtue and abhorred discord in viciousness.
Augustine conceived the rational soul and the nature of truth and the chief good to consist in unity.
Augustine imagined an unknown substance of irrational life and the nature of the chief evil to exist in division.
Augustine called the first principle a Monad, as if it were a soul without sex.
Augustine called the second principle a Duad.
Augustine did not know that evil was not a substance.
Augustine did not know that his soul was not the chief and unchangeable good.
Augustine maintained that God's unchangeable substance erred under constraint rather than confess his own voluntary error.
Augustine was approximately twenty years old when a book of Aristotle called the ten Predicaments fell into his hands.
Augustine read and understood Aristotle's ten Predicaments unaided.
Augustine's rhetoric master of Carthage and others spoke of Aristotle's work with great pride.
Augustine conferred with others who said they scarcely understood the ten Predicaments even with able tutors.
Augustine learned more from reading the ten Predicaments by himself than others learned with tutors explaining it orally and drawing diagrams in sand.
Augustine was proud of his intellectual ability to understand difficult philosophical texts.
Augustine was scarce twenty years old when a book of Aristotle called the ten Predicaments fell into his hands.
Augustine read Aristotle's Predicaments unaided and understood it without instruction.
Augustine's rhetoric master in Carthage spoke of Aristotle's work with great pride.
Augustine understood the liberal arts including rhetoric, logic, geometry, music, and arithmetic without much difficulty or instruction.
Augustine had quickness of understanding and acuteness in discerning as gifts from God.
Augustine did not sacrifice his abilities to God but used them for his own perdition.
Augustine wandered from God into a far country to spend his abilities on harlotries.
Augustine was hurried abroad by the voices of his own errors.
Augustine was sinking into the lowest pit through the weight of his own pride.
Augustine imagined that God was a vast and bright body and that he was a fragment of that body.
Augustine professed blasphemies to men and barked against God.
Augustine was in his nine-and-twentieth year of age when a Bishop of the Manichees named Faustus came to Carthage.
Augustine commended Faustus's smooth language but could separate it from the truth of things he was earnest to learn.
Augustine had read and well remembered much of the philosophers.
Augustine compared the philosophers' teachings with the fables of the Manichees and found the former more probable.
Augustine had read much in the books of secular philosophy about solstices, equinoxes, and eclipses.
Augustine discovered that Manichaeus's teachings about celestial matters did not correspond with calculations and visible testimonies of the stars.
Augustine was commanded to believe Manichaeus's teachings about celestial matters, but they contradicted what he had learned from secular philosophy.
Augustine had been a disciple of the Manichees with an unsettled mind for almost all of nine years.
Augustine longed intensely for the coming of Faustus during those nine years.
Augustine had objections about celestial matters that the rest of the Manichee sect could not solve.
Augustine had his back to the light and his face to things enlightened, so his face was not enlightened.
Augustine did not know whence came all that was true or certain in the liberal arts he read.
Augustine delighted in the liberal arts but did not sacrifice to God for them.
Augustine kept not his strength for God.
Augustine felt that the arts were attained with great difficulty even by the studious and talented.
Augustine attempted to explain the arts to others.
Augustine's wit was nimble in sciences and knotty volumes.
Augustine erred foully and with sacrilegious shamefulness in the doctrine of piety.
Augustine blushed not then to profess blasphemies to men.
Augustine had good abilities but did not employ them to good uses.
Augustine conceived falsehood of God, not truth.
Augustine's conceptions were fictions of his misery, not realities of God's blessedness.
Augustine had his face to things enlightened but was not enlightened himself.
Augustine was the vile slave of vile affections.
Augustine read all the books of the so-called liberal arts that he could procure.
Augustine understood rhetoric, logic, geometry, music, and arithmetic by himself.
Augustine did not blush to confess to God his mercies toward him.
Augustine's bones were not yet humbled.
Augustine did not hear joy and gladness from God's Bridegroom's voice.
Augustine was commanded by God that the earth should bring forth briars and thorns to him.
Augustine was commanded by God that in the sweat of his brows he should eat his bread.
Augustine sought to understand God's wonderful and unchangeable Unity as if God were subjected to His own greatness or beauty.
Augustine imagined that God's greatness and beauty existed in God as their subject, like qualities in bodies.
Augustine believed falsely that God was comprehended under the ten Predicaments.
Augustine had turned away from God.
Augustine's good ever lived with God.
Augustine's firmness when it was God was firmness, but when it was his own it was infirmity.
Augustine's mansion did not fall through his absence from God.
Augustine's soul could praise God and love God.
Augustine's soul could confess God's mercies and praise God.
Augustine was seeking God in his nine-and-twentieth year of age.
God was before Augustine, but Augustine had gone away from God.
Augustine did not find himself, much less God.
Faustus was a Bishop of the Manichees.
Faustus was a great snare of the Devil.
Faustus had smooth language that entangled many.
Faustus was praised among the Manichees as most knowing in all valuable learning.
Faustus was exquisitely skilled in the liberal sciences.
Augustine compared things from the philosophers with the long fables of the Manichees.
The philosophers could only prevail so far as to make judgment of the lower world.
The philosophers could by no means find out the Lord of the lower world.
Manichaeus wrote much on celestial subjects in his frenzy.
Manichaeus's teachings did not contain any account of the solstices, equinoxes, or eclipses of the greater lights.
Manichaeus taught falsely of the heaven and stars and of the motions of the sun and moon.
Manichaeus delivered things which he knew not with mad vanity of pride.
Manichaeus sought to ascribe celestial knowledge to himself as to a divine person.
Manichaeus claimed that the Holy Ghost, the Comforter and Enricher of God's faithful ones, was with plenary authority personally within him.
Augustine could patiently behold a Christian brother ignorant of celestial things and mistaken on them.
Augustine did not see that ignorance as to the position or character of the corporeal creation could injure a believer so long as he did not believe anything unworthy of God.
Augustine saw that it injures a believer if he imagines celestial knowledge pertains to the form of the doctrine of piety and affirms it stiffly while ignorant.
Augustine had not yet clearly ascertained whether the vicissitudes of longer and shorter days and nights could be explained consistently with Manichaeus's sayings.
Augustine might rest his credence upon Faustus's authority on account of his reputed sanctity.
Augustine was a disciple of the Manichees for almost nine years with an unsettled mind.
Augustine longed intensely for the coming of Faustus during his time as a Manichee disciple.
Augustine found Faustus to be a man of pleasing discourse who spoke fluently.
Augustine was not allowed to ask troubling questions in familiar conversation with Faustus in the assembly of his auditors.
Faustus was ignorant of liberal sciences except grammar, which he knew only in an ordinary way.
Faustus had read some of Tully's Orations, a few books of Seneca, some poetry, and some Latin volumes of his own sect.
Faustus was daily practiced in speaking and acquired considerable eloquence.
Augustine began to despair of Faustus solving the difficulties that perplexed him.
Faustus modestly refused to discuss and decide questions about astronomical calculations.
Faustus was not ashamed to confess his ignorance.
Augustine liked Faustus better for his modesty than he would have liked him for knowledge of the things Augustine desired.
Augustine's zeal for the writings of Manichaeus was blunted by his experience with Faustus.
Augustine was a rhetoric-reader teaching young students at Carthage.
Augustine began to study literature with Faustus, which was a subject Faustus was much devoted to.
Augustine did not detach himself from the Manichees altogether after his disappointment with Faustus.
Augustine's mother poured out tears night and day in prayer for him.
Augustine did not wish to go to Rome because of higher gains and dignities, though these things had some influence on his mind.
Augustine's chief reason for going to Rome was that young men studied there more peacefully under regular discipline.
At Carthage, students burst into schools audaciously and disturbed order with frantic gestures.
Augustine would not adopt the disorderly manners of students when he was a student himself.
Augustine was well pleased to go to Rome where he was assured such disorder did not occur.
Augustine's mother grievously bewailed his journey and followed him to the sea.
Augustine deceived his mother by feigning he had a friend he could not leave.
Augustine lied to his mother and escaped to sail to Rome.
Augustine's mother refused to return without him and he scarcely persuaded her to stay the night in a place near the ship.
Augustine privily departed that night while his mother was weeping and praying.
Augustine's mother was frantic with sorrow the next morning when she discovered his departure.
Augustine's mother loved his being with her more than many mothers love their sons.
Augustine was received in Rome by bodily sickness.
Augustine was carrying all the sins he had committed against God, himself, and others when he went to Rome.
Augustine believed Christ to be a phantasm rather than having a true body.
Augustine's fever heightened and he was parting and departing for ever.
Augustine did not desire baptism during his dangerous illness in Rome.
Augustine had begged for baptism from his mother's piety when he was a boy.
Augustine had grown up to his own shame and madly scoffed at the prescripts of God's medicine.
Augustine's mother was a chaste and sober widow.
Augustine's mother was frequent in almsdeeds and full of duty and service to God's saints.
Augustine's mother did not intermit the oblation at God's altar, coming twice a day, morning and evening.
Augustine's mother came to church not for idle tattlings and old wives' fables but to hear God's discourses.
Augustine's mother begged God not for gold or silver but for the salvation of her son's soul.
Augustine recovered from his sickness in Rome.
Augustine joined himself to the Manichees at Rome, both with their disciples and with those called "The Elect."
Augustine believed that it was not he that sinned but some other nature that sinned in him.
Augustine's pride was delighted to be free from blame for his evil actions.
Augustine loved to excuse his evil actions and accuse some other thing that was with him but which he was not.
Augustine did not judge himself a sinner, which made his sin more incurable.
Augustine would rather have God to be overcome in him to his destruction than himself to be saved by God.
Augustine was still united with the Manichee Elect because God had not yet set a watch before his mouth.
Augustine began despairing to make proficiency in the false doctrine of the Manichees.
Augustine held the Manichee doctrines more laxly and carelessly after despairing of proficiency in them.
Augustine began to think that the philosophers called Academics were wiser than others.
Augustine understood the Academics to hold that men ought to doubt everything and that no truth can be comprehended by man.
Augustine was divided against himself by his impiety.
Augustine did not judge himself a sinner.
Augustine preferred God to be overcome in him to his destruction rather than himself to be saved by God.
Augustine was united with those who work iniquity.
Augustine despaired of making proficiency in Manichaean doctrine.
Augustine thought the Academic philosophers were wiser than others because they held that men ought to doubt everything.
Augustine did not understand the meaning of the Academic philosophers.
Augustine discouraged his host from over-confidence in the fables of Manichaeus.
Augustine lived in more familiar friendship with Manichees than with others.
Augustine did not maintain his Manichaean beliefs with his ancient eagerness.
Augustine's intimacy with the Manichaean sect made him slower to seek another way.
Augustine despaired of finding truth in the Church.
Augustine thought it unseemly to believe God had the shape of human flesh.
Augustine could only think of God as a mass of bodies.
Augustine believed Evil to be a substance with its own foul and hideous bulk.
Augustine conceived two masses contrary to one another, with evil narrower and good more expansive.
Augustine thought the Catholic faith could not answer Manichaean objections.
Augustine believed God was unbounded except where the mass of evil opposed Him.
Augustine thought it better to believe God created no evil than that evil came from God.
Augustine believed Christ was reached forth from God's most lucid substance.
Augustine thought Christ could not be born of the Virgin Mary without being mingled with flesh.
Augustine feared believing Christ was born in the flesh lest he believe Him defiled.
Augustine thought the Manichees' criticisms of Scripture could not be defended.
Augustine wished to confer with someone skilled in Scripture about Manichaean objections.
Augustine was stirred by Helpidius's arguments against the Manichees at Carthage.
Augustine found the Manichees' answers to scriptural objections weak.
Augustine was held down by conceptions of corporeal masses.
Augustine came to Rome to teach rhetoric.
Augustine gathered students to his house in Rome.
Augustine found different offences in Rome than in Africa.
Augustine hated youths in Rome who broke faith with their masters to avoid paying stipends.
Augustine hated these youths more because he suffered by them than because they did unlawful things.
Augustine now hates depraved persons though he loves them if corrigible.
Augustine prefers learning to money and God to learning.
Augustine applied to Symmachus, the prefect, to be sent as a rhetoric reader to Milan.
Augustine came to Milan to meet Ambrose the Bishop.
Augustine began to love Ambrose, at first not as a teacher of truth but as a person kind to him.
Augustine listened to Ambrose preaching, trying his eloquence rather than learning the matter.
Augustine was delighted with the sweetness of Ambrose's discourse.
Augustine found Ambrose's discourse less winning and harmonious than Faustus's.
Augustine found no comparison between Faustus's Manichaean delusions and Ambrose's sound teaching of salvation.
Augustine was drawing nearer to truth by little and little unconsciously.
Augustine took no pains to learn what Ambrose spoke but only to hear how he spoke.
Augustine could not separate the words from the things they conveyed.
Augustine began to see that the Catholic faith could be defended.
Augustine heard Old Testament places resolved figuratively.
Augustine blamed his despair in believing no answer could be given to those who hated the Law and Prophets.
Augustine did not think the Catholic way was to be held merely because it could find learned maintainers.
Augustine did not think his Manichaean beliefs were to be condemned merely because both sides could be maintained.
Augustine thought the Catholic cause was not vanquished but not yet victorious.
Augustine could not conceive of spiritual substance.
Augustine judged the tenets of most philosophers more probable than Manichaean tenets.
Augustine settled on doubting everything and wavering between all, after the manner of the Academics.
Augustine abandoned the Manichees, judging he might not continue in that sect.
Augustine preferred some philosophers to the Manichees.
Augustine refused to commit the cure of his sick soul to philosophers without the saving Name of Christ.
Augustine determined to be a Catechumen in the Catholic Church until something certain should dawn upon him.
Augustine's parents had commended him to the Catholic Church.
Augustine walked in darkness and in slippery places.
Augustine sought God abroad out of himself and found not the God of his heart.
Augustine came into the depths of the sea and distrusted and despaired of finding truth.
Augustine's mother followed him over sea and land in all perils.
Augustine's mother was assured of safe arrival by a vision from God.
Augustine's mother found him in grievous peril through despair of finding truth.
Augustine discovered to his mother that he was no longer a Manichee though not yet a Catholic Christian.
Augustine's mother was not overjoyed at his rejection of Manichaeism.
Augustine's mother had bewailed him as one dead.
Augustine's mother believed in Christ that before she departed this life she should see him a Catholic believer.
Augustine's mother poured forth copious prayers and tears to God.
Augustine's mother hastened eagerly to the Church and hung upon the lips of Ambrose.
Augustine's mother loved Ambrose as an angel of God.
Augustine's mother brought cakes, bread and wine to churches built in memory of the Saints in Africa.
Augustine's mother was forbidden by the door-keeper from bringing offerings to the churches.
Augustine's mother embraced the Bishop's wishes when she knew he had forbidden this practice.
Augustine's mother was not seized by wine-bibbing or love of wine.
Augustine's mother brought only one small cup of wine diluted according to her abstemious habits.
Augustine's mother sought devotion, not pleasure, in her church practices.
Augustine's mother willingly forbare the custom of bringing offerings when forbidden by Ambrose.
Augustine's mother learned to bring a breast filled with purified petitions instead of a basket of fruits.
Augustine's mother gave what she could to the poor.
Augustine's mother would not have so readily yielded to cutting off the custom had it been forbidden by another whom she loved not as Ambrose.
Augustine's mother loved Ambrose most entirely for his salvation.
Ambrose loved Augustine's mother for her most religious conversation.
Ambrose was constant at church in good works and fervent in spirit.
Ambrose often burst forth in praises of Augustine's mother when he saw Augustine.
Ambrose congratulated Augustine that he had such a mother.
Augustine did not yet groan in his prayers that God would help him.
Augustine's spirit was wholly intent on learning and restless to dispute.
Augustine esteemed Ambrose a happy man whom personages so great held in such honour.
Augustine thought Ambrose's celibacy seemed a painful course.
Augustine's mother loved Ambrose most entirely for her salvation.
Ambrose was loved by Augustine's mother for her salvation.
Augustine's mother was religious and constant at church.
Augustine's mother was fervent in spirit and constant in good works.
Ambrose often praised Augustine's mother when he saw Augustine.
Augustine doubted all things in his youth.
Augustine imagined the way to life could not be found out.
Augustine did not groan in his prayers for God's help.
Augustine's spirit was wholly intent on learning.
Augustine was restless to dispute.
Augustine esteemed Ambrose a happy man.
Ambrose was held in great honour by personages of great rank.
Augustine could not conjecture what struggles Ambrose had against temptations.
Augustine could not conjecture what comfort Ambrose found in adversities.
Augustine had not experienced the sweet joys of God's Bread.
Ambrose was surrounded by multitudes of busy people whose weaknesses he served.
Ambrose refreshed his body with sustenance absolutely necessary.
Ambrose refreshed his mind with reading.
Ambrose read to himself with his eye gliding over pages and his heart searching out sense.
Ambrose's voice and tongue were at rest while reading.
No man was forbidden to enter Ambrose's presence.
It was not Ambrose's custom to have visitors announced to him.
People would sit silent in Ambrose's presence, not daring to intrude.
Ambrose obtained small intervals free from the din of others' business.
Ambrose used intervals to recruit his mind.
Ambrose may have feared being interrupted while reading.
Ambrose may have feared being asked to expound obscure passages.
Ambrose's voice could be weakened by very little speaking.
Augustine had no opportunity to enquire of Ambrose what he wished.
Augustine heard Ambrose expound the Word of truth every Lord's day.
Augustine was convinced that calumnies against the Divine Books could be unravelled.
Augustine had barked against the Catholic faith for many years.
Augustine had barked against fictions of carnal imaginations.
Augustine had been rash and impious in his youth.
Augustine had pronounced on matters he ought to have enquired about.
Augustine had condemned the Catholic faith insultingly.
Augustine was ignorant of how God's image should subsist.
Augustine should have knocked and proposed his doubt rather than opposed it.
Augustine was delighted by the Catholic Church's sound doctrine.
Augustine was delighted that the Catholic Church did not confine God in space.
Augustine had reviled God's holy ones for thinking God was confined in space.
Augustine had been converted and confounded.
Augustine had been put upon with the name of Christ as an infant.
Augustine now read the old Scriptures of the law and the Prophets with joy.
Augustine had previously reviled those who thought spiritually about Scripture.
Augustine heard Ambrose recommend the text 'The letter killeth, but the Spirit giveth life.'
Ambrose drew aside the mystic veil in his sermons.
Ambrose laid open spiritually what seemed unsound according to the letter.
Augustine kept his heart from assenting to things, fearing to fall headlong.
Augustine was killed by hanging in suspense.
Augustine wished to be as assured of unseen things as he was that seven and three are ten.
Augustine was not mad enough to think that even mathematics could not be comprehended.
Augustine desired to have other things as clear as mathematics.
Augustine desired clarity about corporeal things not present to his senses.
Augustine desired clarity about spiritual things.
Augustine knew not how to conceive of spiritual things except corporeally.
Augustine could have been cured by believing.
Augustine's soul could have been healed by believing.
Augustine refused to be cured, lest he believe falsehoods.
Augustine resisted God's hands.
Augustine preferred the Catholic doctrine as more unassuming and honest.
The Catholic Church required belief in things not demonstrated.
The Manichees mocked Augustine's credulity with a promise of certain knowledge.
The Manichees imposed fabulous and absurd things to be believed.
God touched and composed Augustine's heart with a tender and merciful hand.
Augustine believed innumerable things he did not see.
Augustine believed many things in secular history.
Augustine believed reports of places and cities he had not seen.
Augustine believed reports about friends.
Augustine believed reports about physicians.
Augustine believed reports about other men.
Augustine believed with unshaken assurance about his parents.
Augustine could not have known his parents without believing upon hearsay.
Augustine believed God's Books were established in great authority among almost all nations.
Augustine believed that those who did not believe God's Books were to be blamed.
Augustine believed that God is.
Augustine believed that the government of human things belongs to God.
Augustine sometimes believed more strongly, sometimes more weakly.
Augustine ever believed that God was and had care of us.
Augustine was ignorant of what was to be thought of God's substance.
Augustine was ignorant of what way led or led back to God.
Augustine was too weak by abstract reasonings to find out truth.
Augustine needed the authority of Holy Writ.
Augustine believed God would not have given such excellency of authority to Scripture in all lands unless He willed thereby to be believed in.
Augustine was offended by things sounding strangely in Scripture.
Augustine heard divers things in Scripture expounded satisfactorily.
Augustine referred strange things in Scripture to the depth of mysteries.
Augustine found Scripture's authority more venerable and worthy of religious credence.
Scripture lay open to all to read.
Scripture reserved the majesty of its mysteries within its profounder meaning.
Scripture stooped to all in great plainness of its words and lowliness of its style.
Scripture called forth the intensest application of those not light of heart.
Scripture received all in its open bosom.
Scripture wafted some few towards God through narrow passages.
Augustine panted after honours, gains, and marriage.
God derided Augustine's desires.
Augustine underwent most bitter crosses in his desires.
God was gracious to Augustine by not suffering aught to grow sweet to him which was not God.
Augustine's soul was held by the birdlime of death.
God irritated the feeling of Augustine's soul's wound.
Augustine wandered through the broad way of the world.
God did not forsake Augustine.
Augustine was preparing to recite a panegyric of the Emperor.
Augustine was to utter many lies in the panegyric.
Augustine was to be applauded by those who knew he lied.
Augustine's heart was panting with anxieties.
Augustine's heart was boiling with feverishness of consuming thoughts.
Augustine observed a poor beggar in the streets of Milan.
The beggar had a full belly, was joking and joyous.
Augustine sighed at the sight of the beggar.
Augustine spoke to friends around him of the many sorrows of their frenzies.
Augustine was dragging along under the goading of desire.
Augustine was augmenting the burden of his own wretchedness by dragging.
Augustine looked to arrive at the joyousness the beggar had arrived at before him.
The beggar had obtained joyousness by means of a few begged pence.
Augustine was plotting for joyousness by many toilsome turnings and windings.
Both Augustine and the beggar sought the joy of temporary felicity.
The beggar did not have true joy.
Augustine with his ambitious designs was seeking a joy much less true.
The beggar was joyous.
Augustine was anxious.
The beggar was void of care.
Augustine was full of fears.
Augustine would choose to be merry rather than fearful.
Augustine would choose to be himself rather than like the beggar.
Augustine was worn with cares and fears.
Augustine made this choice out of wrong judgment.
Augustine had no joy in his learning.
Augustine sought to please men by his learning.
Augustine sought to please men simply, not to instruct.
God broke Augustine's bones with the staff of His correction.
The beggar joyed in drunkenness.
Augustine desired to joy in glory.
Augustine's glory was not in God.
The beggar's joy was not true joy.
Augustine's glory was not true glory.
Augustine's false glory overthrew his soul more than the beggar's drunkenness.
The beggar digested his drunkenness that very night.
Augustine slept and rose again with his cares.
Augustine was to sleep again and rise again with his cares.
The joy of a faithful hope lies incomparably beyond vanity.
The beggar was happier than Augustine.
The beggar was thoroughly drenched in mirth.
Augustine was disembowelled with cares.
The beggar had gotten wine by fair wishes.
Augustine was seeking empty, swelling praise by lying.
Augustine often marked how it fared with his friends.
Augustine found it went ill with him.
Augustine grieved and doubled that very ill.
When prosperity smiled on Augustine, he was loth to catch at it.
Prosperity flew away from Augustine almost before he could grasp it.
Augustine and his friends bemoaned these things together.
Augustine spoke most familiarly with Alypius and Nebridius.
Alypius was born in the same town as Augustine.
Alypius was of persons of chief rank in his town.
Alypius was younger than Augustine.
Alypius had studied under Augustine.
Alypius studied under Augustine when Augustine first lectured in their town.
Alypius studied under Augustine afterwards at Carthage.
Alypius loved Augustine much.
Alypius thought Augustine was kind and learned.
Augustine loved Alypius for his great towardliness to virtue.
Alypius's towardliness to virtue was eminent for one of his years.
The whirlpool of Carthaginian habits had drawn Alypius into the madness of the Circus.
Idle spectacles are hotly followed in Carthage.
Alypius was miserably tossed in the madness of the Circus.
Augustine professed rhetoric at Carthage.
Augustine had a public school at Carthage.
Alypius did not use Augustine's teaching.
Unkindness had risen between Alypius's father and Augustine.
Augustine found that Alypius doted deadly upon the Circus.
Augustine was deeply grieved that Alypius seemed likely to throw away so great promise.
Augustine had no means of advising Alypius.
Augustine had no means of reclaiming Alypius with constraint.
Augustine could not reclaim Alypius by the kindness of a friend.
Augustine could not reclaim Alypius by the authority of a master.
Augustine supposed that Alypius thought of him as his father did.
Alypius was not such as his father.
Alypius laid aside his father's mind in that matter.
Alypius began to greet Augustine.
Alypius came sometimes into Augustine's lecture room.
Alypius heard a little and was gone.
Augustine had forgotten to deal with Alypius.
Augustine had forgotten to prevent Alypius from undoing his good wit through blind and headlong desire of vain pastimes.
God guideth the course of all He hath created.
God had not forgotten Alypius.
Alypius was one day to be among God's children.
Alypius was one day to be a Priest and Dispenser of God's Sacrament.
God effected Alypius's amendment through Augustine, unknowingly.
Augustine sat in his accustomed place with his scholars before him.
Alypius entered, greeted Augustine, sat down, and applied his mind to what Augustine handled.
Augustine had by chance a passage in hand.
A likeness from the Circensian races occurred to Augustine while explaining.
Augustine used the likeness to make what he conveyed pleasanter and plainer.
Augustine seasoned the likeness with biting mockery of those enthralled by the madness of the Circus.
Augustine did not then think of curing Alypius of the infection of the Circus.
Alypius took the likeness wholly to himself.
Alypius thought Augustine said it simply for his sake.
Augustine sat in his accustomed place as a lecturer with scholars before him.
Augustine explained passages and used likenesses from Circensian races to make his teaching clearer.
Augustine seasoned his teaching with biting mockery of those enthralled by the madness of the races.
Augustine did not intentionally think of curing Alypius of his addiction to the races.
Alypius took Augustine's remarks personally and thought they were directed at him.
Alypius was a right-minded youth who took offense at himself rather than at Augustine.
Alypius loved Augustine more fervently after this incident.
Alypius burst out of the pit of Circensian pastimes after hearing Augustine's speech.
Alypius was blinded by the wretched pastimes of the Circensian races.
Alypius shook his mind with strong self-command and freed himself from the filths of the Circensian pastimes.
Alypius prevailed with his unwilling father to allow him to be Augustine's scholar.
Alypius became Augustine's hearer again and was involved in the Manichee superstition with Augustine.
Alypius loved in the Manichees what he supposed was true and unfeigned continency.
Alypius did not forsake the secular course his parents had charmed him to pursue.
Alypius went to Rome to study law before Augustine.
Alypius was carried away with incredible eagerness after the shows of gladiators in Rome.
Alypius was utterly averse to and detesting spectacles before being taken to the Amphitheatre.
Alypius was met by acquaintances and fellow-students coming from dinner who haled him to the Amphitheatre.
Alypius resisted and protested being taken to the Amphitheatre.
Alypius said he would be absent while present and would overcome both his companions and the spectacles.
Alypius closed the passage of his eyes when he first arrived at the Amphitheatre.
Alypius forbade his mind to range abroad after evil at the Amphitheatre.
Alypius opened his eyes when a mighty cry struck him during a fight.
Alypius was stricken with a deeper wound in his soul than the gladiator who fell.
Alypius drank down savageness when he saw blood.
Alypius fixed his eye and drank in frenzy unawares at the gladiatorial shows.
Alypius was delighted with the guilty fight and intoxicated with the bloody pastime.
Alypius was no longer the man he came as but became one of the throng.
Alypius shouted, kindled, and was carried away with madness to return to the shows.
Alypius was plucked from the gladiatorial madness by God's strong and merciful hand.
God taught Alypius to have confidence not in himself but in God.
Alypius was studying under Augustine at Carthage when he was apprehended by market-place officers for theft.
Alypius was thinking over at mid-day in the market-place what he was to say by heart.
Alypius was walking up and down by himself before the judgment-seat with his note-book and pen.
Alypius found a hatchet in the silversmiths' shops and was standing wondering and considering it.
Alypius was seized by market-place officers who found him alone with the hatchet.
Alypius was being led away to be taken before the judge when an architect met them.
The architect had seen Alypius at a certain senator's house and recognized him immediately.
The architect took Alypius aside by the hand and enquired the occasion of his calamity.
The architect bade all present to go with him to the house of the young man who had committed the theft.
A boy at the young man's house disclosed that the hatchet was his master's.
The crime was transferred to the young man's house and the multitude was ashamed.
Alypius went away better experienced and instructed after being falsely accused.
Augustine found Alypius at Rome.
Alypius clave to Augustine by a most strong tie.
Alypius went with Augustine to Milan.
Alypius went to Milan both to not leave Augustine and to practise law he had studied.
Alypius practised law more to please his parents than himself.
Alypius sat thrice as Assessor with uncorruptness much wondered at by others.
Alypius wondered at others rather who could prefer gold to honesty.
Alypius was Assessor to the count of the Italian Treasury at Rome.
A very powerful senator tried to have Alypius allow something unallowed by law.
Alypius resisted the powerful senator's demands.
A bribe was promised to Alypius and he scorned it with all his heart.
Threats were held out to Alypius and he trampled upon them.
Alypius neither desired the friendship nor feared the enmity of the powerful senator.
The judge whose councillor Alypius was put the matter off upon Alypius, alleging he would not allow him to do it.
Alypius was nearly seduced to have books copied for him at Praetorian prices.
Alypius consulted justice and altered his deliberation for the better.
Alypius esteemed equity more gainful than the power he was offered.
Alypius cleaved to Augustine at that time.
Alypius wavered with Augustine in purpose about what course of life was to be taken.
Nebridius left his native country near Carthage.
Nebridius left Carthage where he had much lived.
Nebridius left his excellent family-estate and house.
Nebridius left his mother behind who was not to follow him.
Nebridius came to Milan for no other reason but to live with Augustine in a most ardent search after truth and wisdom.
Nebridius sighed like Augustine.
Nebridius wavered like Augustine.
Nebridius was an ardent searcher after true life.
Nebridius was a most acute examiner of the most difficult questions.
Augustine, Alypius, and Nebridius were three indigent persons sighing out their wants one to another.
Augustine, Alypius, and Nebridius waited upon God that He might give them their meat in due season.
Augustine and his companions looked towards the end of their worldly affairs but darkness met them.
Augustine and his companions turned away groaning and asking how long these things would be.
Augustine and his companions often said these things but did not forsake them.
Augustine began to kindle with the desire of wisdom at his nineteenth year.
Augustine was in his thirtieth year when he was still sticking in the same mire.
Augustine was greedy of enjoying things present which passed away and wasted his soul.
Augustine said to himself that tomorrow he would find wisdom and it would appear manifestly.
Augustine expected Faustus the Manichee to come and clear everything.
Augustine wondered whether the Academicians were right that no certainty can be attained for the ordering of life.
Augustine found that things in the ecclesiastical books were not absurd to him.
Augustine decided to take his stand where his parents had placed him as a child until the clear truth be found out.
Augustine noted that Ambrose had no leisure.
Augustine and his companions had no leisure to read.
Augustine wondered where they would find books and when they would procure them.
Augustine noted that the forenoons were taken up by scholars.
Augustine wondered when they would pay court to great friends whose favour they needed.
Augustine wondered when they would compose what they could sell to scholars.
Augustine wondered when they would refresh themselves unbending their minds from the intenseness of care.
Augustine said that life is vain and death uncertain.
Augustine feared that death might steal upon them suddenly.
Augustine wondered in what state they would depart if death came suddenly.
Augustine believed that the excellent dignity of the authority of the Christian Faith had overspread the whole world.
Augustine believed that God would not work such great things if with the death of the body the life of the soul came to an end.
Augustine found that worldly things were pleasant and had some and no small sweetness.
Augustine thought it would be a shame to return to worldly things after abandoning them.
Augustine thought it was no great matter to obtain some station.
Augustine had store of powerful friends.
Augustine thought a presidentship might be given to him.
Augustine thought of taking a wife with some money that she increase not his charges.
Augustine thought this would be the bound of his desire.
Augustine noted that many great men and most worthy of imitation had given themselves to the study of wisdom in the state of marriage.
Augustine's heart was shifted and driven this way and that by conflicting desires.
Augustine delayed to turn to the Lord.
Augustine deferred from day to day to live in God and deferred not daily to die in himself.
Augustine loved a happy life but feared it in its own abode.
Augustine sought a happy life by fleeing from it.
Augustine thought he would be too miserable unless folded in female arms.
Augustine did not think of the medicine of God's mercy to cure his infirmity.
Augustine had not tried the medicine of God's mercy.
Augustine supposed continency to be in his own power.
Augustine did not find the power of continency in himself.
Augustine was foolish enough not to know that none can be continent unless God gives it.
Augustine did not know that God would give continency if he knocked at God's ears with inward groanings and cast his care on God with settled faith.
Alypius kept Augustine from marrying.
Alypius alleged that Augustine and he could not with undistracted leisure live together in the love of wisdom if Augustine married.
Alypius was most pure in the matter of continency.
Alypius had entered into a course of sensuality in the outset of his youth.
Alypius had not stuck fast in sensuality.
Alypius had felt remorse and revolting at sensuality.
Alypius lived most continently from that time until the present.
Augustine opposed Alypius with examples of married men who had cherished wisdom and served God acceptably.
Augustine noted that married men had retained their friends and loved them faithfully.
Augustine was far short of the greatness of spirit of married men who cherished wisdom.
Augustine was bound with the disease of the flesh and its deadly sweetness.
Augustine drew along his chain dreading to be loosed.
Augustine put back Alypius's good persuasions as if they were the hand of one that would unchain him.
Augustine spoke to Alypius by his tongue weaving and laying pleasurable snares in his path.
Augustine's tongue wove snares wherein Alypius's virtuous and free feet might be entangled.
Alypius wondered that Augustine, whom he esteemed not slightly, should stick so fast in the birdlime.
Augustine desired to live with undistracted leisure in the love of wisdom.
Alypius was exceptionally pure regarding sexual matters.
Alypius entered into a course of sexual conduct in his youth but did not persist in it.
Alypius felt remorse and revulsion about his youthful sexual conduct.
Alypius lived continently from that point forward.
Augustine opposed Alypius's desire for celibacy by citing examples of married men who cherished wisdom and served God.
Augustine was bound by the disease of the flesh and its deadly sweetness.
Augustine dreaded being loosed from his fleshly chains.
Augustine discouraged Alypius from pursuing celibacy through his own example and arguments.
Alypius wondered at Augustine's inability to lead a single life.
Augustine claimed he could never lead a single life.
Alypius began to desire marriage out of curiosity rather than desire for pleasure.
Alypius wanted to understand what made Augustine's life seem unbearable without marriage.
Augustine's mother worked to arrange his marriage.
Augustine was promised a maiden in marriage who was two years below marriageable age.
Augustine and his friends debated living apart from business and the bustle of men.
Augustine and his friends planned to create a communal household where all property would be shared.
Romanianus was Augustine's childhood friend and the most earnest advocate for the communal living project.
Romanianus had an estate that far exceeded those of the other friends.
The communal living plan failed when the friends considered whether their wives would permit it.
Augustine had a concubine who was separated from him as a hindrance to his marriage.
Augustine's heart was wounded when his concubine was torn from his side.
Augustine's concubine returned to Africa vowing never to know another man.
Augustine had a son with his concubine.
Augustine could not imitate his concubine's continence and procured another concubine while waiting for his promised bride.
Augustine was a slave to lust rather than a true lover of marriage.
Augustine's wound from losing his first concubine became mortified and more desperate.
The fear of death and God's judgment never departed from Augustine's breast.
Augustine disputed with friends Alypius and Nebridius about the nature of good and evil.
Augustine believed Epicurus had won the philosophical debate, except for his belief in an afterlife.
Augustine could not be happy without friends, even amid carnal pleasures.
Augustine loved his friends for themselves only.
Augustine felt he was beloved by his friends for himself only.
Augustine passed from evil youth into early manhood.
Augustine became more defiled by vain things as he grew in years.
Augustine could not conceive of any substance except what is seen with physical eyes.
Augustine avoided conceiving of God under the figure of a human body.
Augustine believed God was incorruptible, uninjurable, and unchangeable.
Augustine preferred the incorruptible to the corruptible.
Augustine preferred the uninjurable to the injurable.
Augustine preferred the unchangeable to things subject to change.
Augustine struggled to conceive of God except as being in space.
Augustine thought anything not extended over space was nothing.
Augustine attempted to conceive of God as vast and penetrating infinite spaces.
Augustine compared God's presence to light passing through air.
Augustine's conception of God as spatially distributed was false.
Augustine opposed the Manichees and their doctrine of a nation of darkness opposing God.
Nebridius proposed an argument against the Manichees that Augustine found compelling.
Augustine held that God was undefilable and unalterable.
Augustine struggled to understand the cause of evil.
Augustine perceived that free will was the cause of doing evil.
Augustine perceived that God's just judgment was the cause of suffering evil.
Augustine knew he had a will and that he lived.
Augustine was certain that when he willed or refused something, no other than himself did so.
Augustine judged that what he did against his will was suffering rather than doing.
Augustine confessed himself to be justly punished by God.
Augustine questioned who made him and how he came to will evil.
Augustine was confused about the origin of evil will in himself and in the devil.
Augustine strove to find the cause of evil without believing God suffered ill.
Augustine was formed by God.
Augustine struggled with the problem of evil's origin.
Augustine believed God is incorruptible.
Augustine believed God is the sovereign and best good.
Augustine believed corruption does not impair God's substance.
Augustine believed God's will and power are identical to God Himself.
Augustine sought to understand the origin of evil.
Augustine visualized creation as a finite mass within an infinite God.
Augustine initially professed faith in Christ and the Catholic Church.
Augustine's faith was firm but unformed and fluctuating in some points.
Augustine rejected astrology and divination.
Vindicianus was an acute old man who opposed astrology.
Nebridius was a young man of admirable talents who opposed astrology.
Firminus was Augustine's dear friend and a skilled rhetorician.
Firminus consulted Augustine about astrological predictions.
Augustine had begun to incline toward Nebridius' opinion against astrology.
Firminus' father was curious about astrological books.
Firminus and a slave were born at the same moment with identical constellations.
Firminus was born into a high estate and lived a life of wealth and honor.
The slave born at the same moment as Firminus remained in servitude.
Augustine used the example of Firminus and the slave to refute astrology.
Augustine argued that twins born at nearly the same moment have different fates.
Augustine cited Esau and Jacob as examples of twins with different destinies.
Augustine believed astrologers speak truly by chance, not by art.
Augustine believed God's hidden inspiration guides what consulters hear.
Augustine believed God judges men.
Augustine believed Christ is God's Son and the way of salvation.
Augustine believed the holy Scriptures contain the way of man's salvation.
Augustine believed the Catholic Church's authority pressed the Scriptures upon him.
Augustine believed in life after death.
Augustine suffered greatly while seeking the origin of evil.
Augustine's internal struggles were not fully communicated to his closest friends.
Augustine believed his desires were known to God.
Augustine believed he was superior to material things but inferior to God.
Augustine believed true joy comes from being subjected to God.
Augustine believed he should rule his body by serving God.
Augustine had risen proudly against God.
Augustine's pride separated him from God.
Augustine's pride-swollen face closed his eyes spiritually.
Augustine believed God humbles the proud.
Augustine believed God pitied his dust and ashes.
Augustine believed God used inward goads to make him ill at ease.
Augustine's swelling was abated by God's secret medicining.
Augustine's troubled mind was healed by smarting anointings of healthful sorrows.
Augustine believed God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble.
Augustine believed God traced out the way of humility through the Word made flesh.
Augustine received books of the Platonists translated from Greek into Latin.
Augustine read in the Platonists ideas similar to the opening of the Gospel of John.
Augustine found in the Platonists that the Word was God and all things were made by Him.
Augustine found in the Platonists that the Word is the true light that enlightens every man.
Augustine did not find in the Platonists that the Word was made flesh.
Augustine did not find in the Platonists that Christ came unto His own.
Augustine did not find in the Platonists that believers receive power to become sons of God.
Augustine read philosophical texts that described God the Word as being in the form of the Father and equal with God.
Augustine did not find in those philosophical texts the doctrine that the Word was made flesh and dwelt among us.
Augustine did not find in those texts the teaching that Christ emptied Himself and took the form of a servant.
Augustine did not find in those texts that Christ died for the ungodly or that God delivered His Only Son for humanity.
Augustine found that those texts taught souls receive from the Son's fullness and are blessed through participation in wisdom.
Augustine came to God from among the Gentiles.
Augustine set his mind upon the gold which God willed His people to take from Egypt.
Augustine did not set his mind on the idols of Egypt.
Augustine was admonished to return to himself and entered into his inward self with God as his Guide.
Augustine beheld with the eye of his soul a Light Unchangeable above his mind.
Augustine perceived himself to be far off from God in the region of unlikeness.
Augustine learned that God chastens man for iniquity.
Augustine heard God's voice saying 'I am the food of grown men.'
Augustine was more certain that Truth exists than that he himself lives.
Augustine perceived that things below God neither altogether are nor altogether are not.
Augustine held that it is good to hold fast unto God.
Augustine came to understand that evil is not any substance but a perversion of the will turned away from God.
Augustine wondered that he now loved God and not a phantasm for God.
Augustine was borne up to God by God's beauty but soon borne down by his own weight.
Augustine's weight was carnal custom.
Augustine had a remembrance of God dwelling with him.
Augustine doubted not that there was One to whom he might cleave but was not yet such as to cleave to God.
Augustine was certain that God's invisible works are clearly seen and understood by the things that are made.
Augustine examined whence it was that he admired the beauty of bodies celestial and terrestrial.
Augustine found the unchangeable and true Eternity of Truth above his changeable mind.
Augustine passed by degrees from bodies to the soul, from the soul to its inward faculty, and from there to the reasoning faculty.
Augustine's reasoning faculty raised itself up to its own understanding and drew away his thoughts from the power of habit.
Augustine arrived at the perception of THAT WHICH IS through a flash of one trembling glance.
Augustine could not fix his gaze upon God and was thrown back on his wonted habits.
Augustine carried along a loving memory of his perception of God and a longing for what he had perceived the odour of.
Augustine sought a way of obtaining strength sufficient to enjoy God.
Augustine found the way to strength in embracing Christ Jesus, the Mediator betwixt God and men.
Augustine did not initially hold to his Lord Jesus Christ, the Humble.
Augustine did not yet know whereto Christ's infirmity would guide him.
Augustine thought of Christ only as a man of excellent wisdom.
Augustine knew that Christ was wonderfully born of a Virgin.
Augustine believed Christ attained great eminence of authority as an example of despising things temporal for obtaining immortality.
Augustine could not imagine the mystery in 'The Word was made flesh.'
Augustine had learned from written accounts that Christ ate, drank, slept, walked, rejoiced in spirit, was sorrowful, and discoursed.
Augustine initially conceived of Christ only as a man of excellent wisdom.
Augustine believed Christ was wonderfully born of a Virgin.
Augustine learned that Christ ate, drank, slept, walked, rejoiced, was sorrowful, and discoursed.
Augustine acknowledged that Christ was a perfect man with body, sensitive soul, and rational mind.
Alypius initially believed the Catholics thought God was clothed with flesh without a soul in Christ.
Alypius moved slowly towards Christian faith due to his misunderstanding about Christ's nature.
Alypius later learned that the Apollinarian heresy was his error and was conformed to Catholic faith.
Augustine read books of the Platonists and was taught to search for incorporeal truth.
Augustine perceived God's invisible nature through things made.
Augustine was assured that God is infinite, unchanging, and the source of all things.
Augustine was too unsure to enjoy God despite being assured of His existence.
Augustine prated as one well skilled but was filled with his own punishment.
Augustine did not mourn but rather scorned, puffed up with knowledge.
Augustine lacked charity building upon the foundation of humility.
Augustine seized the Scriptures and chiefly studied the Apostle Paul.
Augustine found that Paul's apparent contradictions vanished when properly understood.
Augustine learned to rejoice with trembling upon reading Scripture.
Augustine was enthralled with the love of woman.
Augustine chose marriage rather than celibacy, considering himself weak.
Augustine was tossed up and down and wasted with withering cares because of his married life.
Augustine had surmounted the vanity of not knowing God.
Augustine had fallen into the ungodliness of knowing God but not glorifying Him.
Augustine went to Simplicianus for spiritual guidance.
Augustine had read books of the Platonists translated by Victorinus.
Victorinus was a Rhetoric Professor of Rome who died a Christian.
Victorinus was most learned and skilled in the liberal sciences.
Victorinus had a statue erected in the Roman Forum.
Victorinus was a worshipper of idols and participated in sacrilegious rites.
Victorinus had defended pagan gods with thundering eloquence for many years.
Victorinus read holy Scripture most studiously and searched into Christian writings.
Victorinus privately told Simplicianus he was already a Christian.
Victorinus feared offending his proud daemon-worshipping friends.
Victorinus feared being denied by Christ before the holy angels if he did not confess Him before men.
Victorinus became bold-faced against vanity and shame-faced towards truth.
Victorinus asked Simplicianus to go to the Church and be made a Christian.
Victorinus became a Catechumen and later received baptism.
Rome wondered and the Church rejoiced at Victorinus's conversion.
The proud saw Victorinus's conversion and were wroth.
Victorinus chose to profess his faith publicly rather than privately.
Victorinus reasoned that he should not fear the meek flock when pronouncing God's word.
Victorinus made his profession of faith publicly before the holy multitude rather than privately.
Victorinus had previously taught rhetoric publicly.
Victorinus pronounced the true faith with excellent boldness.
The congregation rejoiced greatly at Victorinus's conversion.
Augustine was inspired by Victorinus's example to imitate him.
During the reign of Emperor Julian, a law was made forbidding Christians to teach the liberal sciences or oratory.
Victorinus obeyed the law forbidding Christian teaching and gave up his school rather than abandon God's Word.
Augustine was bound by his own iron will and could not overcome his former willfulness.
Augustine experienced a struggle between his carnal will and his spiritual will.
Augustine's flesh lusted against the spirit and the spirit against the flesh.
Augustine knew the truth but still refused to serve God and feared being freed of worldly encumbrances.
Augustine delayed his conversion, repeatedly saying 'Anon, anon' and 'presently, presently.'
Augustine attended Church whenever free from his business.
Augustine was doing his wonted business and daily sighing unto God.
Alypius was Augustine's companion and had recently been released from his law business.
Alypius was awaiting to sell his counsel as Augustine sold the skill of speaking.
Nebridius was a friend of Augustine and Alypius.
Nebridius consented to teach under Verecundus, a grammarian of Milan.
Verecundus was a citizen and grammarian of Milan and an intimate friend of Augustine's circle.
Nebridius was not motivated by desire for advantage in his teaching position.
Nebridius desired to have free time to seek, read, or hear something concerning wisdom.
Pontitianus was an African countryman of Augustine.
Pontitianus held high office in the Emperor's court.
Pontitianus was a Christian and baptised.
Pontitianus frequently bowed himself before God in the Church in continued prayers.
Augustine bestowed very great pains upon the Scriptures.
Augustine and Alypius were previously ignorant of Antony the Egyptian monk.
Antony had high reputation among God's servants.
There was a monastery at Milan full of good brethren under the fostering care of Ambrose.
Augustine and Alypius did not know of the monastery at Milan.
Pontitianus told of wonderful works wrought in the true Faith and Church Catholic in recent times.
Pontitianus and three companions went to walk in gardens near the city walls at Triers.
While walking in pairs, two of Pontitianus's companions found a cottage inhabited by God's servants.
The companions found a little book containing the life of Antony in the cottage.
One of Pontitianus's companions read the life of Antony and was kindled by it.
The companion who read about Antony meditated on taking up such a life and giving over his secular service.
Two of Pontitianus's companions were agents for the public affairs.
One companion became filled with holy love and sober shame and resolved to serve God.
The companion who resolved to serve God said he would begin serving God from that hour in that place.
The other companion agreed to cleave to his friend and serve God with him.
Both companions forsook all that they had and followed God.
Augustine read Cicero's Hortensius at age nineteen and was stirred to an earnest love of wisdom.
Augustine deferred for approximately twelve years after reading Cicero's Hortensius before rejecting earthly felicity.
Augustine begged God for chastity and continency but asked that it not be given to him yet.
Augustine feared that God would cure him soon of the disease of concupiscence, which he wished to have satisfied rather than extinguished.
Augustine wandered through crooked ways in a sacrilegious superstition.
Augustine deferred from day to day rejecting the hopes of the world because there did not appear anything certain to direct his course.
Augustine had a friend named Alypius.
Augustine and Alypius had use of a garden at their lodging.
Augustine experienced a conflict between his will to serve God and his attachment to worldly pleasures.
Augustine's body obeyed the weakest willing of his soul more easily than his soul obeyed itself.
Augustine experienced two conflicting wills within himself regarding whether to serve God.
Augustine was tormented and soul-sick, accusing himself severely.
Augustine said to himself 'Be it done now, be it done now' but did not immediately enact his resolution.
Augustine hesitated to die to death and to live to life.
Augustine was more accustomed to worse things than to better things.
Augustine's ancient mistresses (worldly pleasures) whispered to him as he was departing from them.
Augustine trembled to go toward the chaste dignity of Continency.
Pontitianus told Augustine a story about two men who read a book and were changed inwardly.
The two men in Pontitianus's story determined to serve God and broke loose from their former hopes.
The two men in Pontitianus's story had affianced brides who dedicated their virginity to God upon hearing of their resolution.
Pontitianus and his companion reminded the two men to return because the day was far spent.
The two men in Pontitianus's story fixed their hearts on heaven and remained in the cottage.
Pontitianus and his companion went away to the palace with hearts lingering on the earth.
Augustine abhored himself when compared with those whose healthful affections he heard of.
Augustine loved those who had resigned themselves wholly to God to be cured.
Augustine had known his iniquity but made as though he saw it not and forgot it.
Augustine's conscience upbraided him, asking where his tongue was that had said it liked not to cast off the baggage of vanity.
Augustine's unlearned people started up and took heaven by force while he and his learning wallowed in flesh and blood.
Augustine's fever of mind tore him away from Alypius.
Augustine's forehead, cheeks, eyes, colour, and tone of voice spoke his mind more than his words.
Augustine was healthfully distracted and dying to live.
Augustine knew what evil thing he was but did not know what good thing he was shortly to become.
Augustine was most vehemently indignant that he entered not into God's will and covenant.
Augustine's bones cried out unto him to enter into God's will and covenant.
Augustine had a maimed and half-divided will, struggling with one part sinking as another rose.
Augustine tore his hair, beat his forehead, and locked his fingers to clasp his knee in the fever of his irresoluteness.
Augustine's body obeyed his will in moving its limbs.
Augustine did not do what he longed incomparably more to do.
Augustine's mind commanded itself to will but was resisted.
Augustine partly willed and partly nilled regarding serving God.
Augustine was at strife with himself and rent asunder by himself.
Augustine's rent befell him against his will.
