Augustine indicated the punishment of her own mind, not the presence of another mind.
Sin dwelt in Augustine as the punishment of a sin more freely committed, in that he was a son of Adam.
Augustine was soul-sick and tormented, rolling and turning in his chain.
Augustine kept his stand hard by and took breath after saying 'Be it done now, be it done now.'.
Augustine essayed again and wanted somewhat less of the resistance each time.
Augustine all but touched and laid hold of his resolution but came not at it.
The worse whereto Augustine was inured prevailed more with him than the better whereto he was unused.
The moment wherein Augustine was to become other than he was struck greater horror into him as it approached.
The toys of toys and vanities of vanities were Augustine's ancient mistresses.
Augustine's ancient mistresses plucked his fleshy garment and whispered softly to him.
Augustine's ancient mistresses suggested defilements and shame to him.
Augustine much less than half heard his ancient mistresses.
Augustine's ancient mistresses muttered behind his back and privily plucked him as he was departing.
Augustine's ancient mistresses retarded him so that he hesitated to burst and shake himself free from them.
A violent habit said to Augustine 'Thinkest thou, thou canst live without them?'.
The violent habit spoke very faintly to Augustine.
The chaste dignity of Continency appeared unto Augustine as serene yet not relaxedly gay.
The chaste dignity of Continency honestly allured Augustine to come and doubt not.
The chaste dignity of Continency stretched forth her holy hands to receive and embrace Augustine.
Augustine experienced internal conflict between his sinful habits and his desire for continence.
Augustine heard a child's voice chanting 'Take up and read' from a neighboring house.
Augustine interpreted the voice as a command from God to open a book and read.
Augustine had heard the story of Saint Antony's conversion through reading the Gospel.
Augustine read a passage from the Apostle about not living in rioting, drunkenness, chambering, wantonness, strife, and envying.
Augustine experienced immediate spiritual transformation and clarity after reading the passage.
Augustine's friend Alypius was sitting beside him during his conversion experience.
Alypius read further in the text and found a passage about receiving those weak in faith.
Alypius applied the passage to himself and was strengthened by it.
Alypius joined Augustine in his conversion without turbulent delay.
Augustine's mother rejoiced when he told her of his conversion.
Augustine's mother had previously seen Augustine in a vision where God showed him to her.
Augustine's mother had prayed for his conversion with pitiful and sorrowful groanings.
Augustine sought neither wife nor hope of worldly things after his conversion.
Augustine resolved to withdraw from his profession as a teacher of rhetoric.
Augustine's lungs were damaged from excessive literary labor.
Augustine experienced difficulty breathing and chest pain from his work.
Augustine waited until the vacation of the vintage before leaving his teaching position.
Augustine and his companions kept their conversion purpose secret from most people.
Augustine was concerned that leaving his position too early would appear like ostentation.
Augustine rejoiced that his lung illness provided a legitimate excuse to leave teaching.
Augustine endured about twenty days in his teaching position after his conversion.
Verecundus was a friend who was not yet Christian but whose wife was faithful.
Verecundus was bound by chains that prevented him from joining Augustine's journey.
Verecundus offered his country-house at Cassiacum to Augustine and his companions.
Verecundus later became Christian while ill and died.
Nebridius was a friend who initially believed the flesh of Christ was a phantom.
Nebridius later emerged from his error and believed as Augustine did.
Nebridius became a faithful member of the Church Catholic after Augustine's conversion.
Nebridius served in perfect chastity and continence among his people in Africa.
Nebridius's entire household became Christian through his influence.
Augustine felt that continence was not barren but a fruitful mother of joys.
Augustine experienced the sweetness of renouncing worldly pleasures.
Augustine's soul became free from the cares of canvassing, getting, and lust.
Augustine resolved to gently withdraw from his profession rather than tear away from it abruptly.
Augustine feared that his early departure would cause offense to those who wanted him to continue teaching.
Augustine believed God had pardoned his sins in holy water.
